output.final_deliverable:
Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page (extracted links from the visit result). I list 10 links found on that Main Page extract. For each item I show: (A) URL (full), (B) link text/title if the visit extract provided it (otherwise I note it was not provided and give an inferred title based on the URL), and (C) a 1-2 sentence description based only on evidence in the merged visit results (or, if no description text was present, I explicitly state that and give an inference). 1. URL: https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/List_of_Wikipedias
   - Link text/title: not provided in extracted data (inferred from URL: "List of Wikipedias").
   - Description: No descriptive text for this link was present in the extracted Main Page content; inferred from the URL that this page is a (meta) list of the various language Wikipedias.

2. URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contents/Portals
   - Link text/title: not explicitly provided in the extracted data (inferred from URL: "Wikipedia:Contents/Portals").
   - Description: The extracted link list included this page name; the visit data did not include a descriptive blurb, so the page appears to be a subpage under "Wikipedia:Contents" related to portals (inferred from the URL).

3. URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contents
   - Link text/title: not explicitly provided in the extracted data (inferred from URL: "Wikipedia:Contents").
   - Description: The Main Page extract listed this internal Wikipedia page; no further descriptive text was present in the extract, so the page likely relates to Wikipedia's contents/organizational pages.

4. URL: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:Main_Page
   - Link text/title: not explicitly provided in the extracted data (inferred from URL: "Wikidata:Main_Page").
   - Description: "Wikidata:Main_Page" was present among the extracted links. The visit extract shows Wikidata links on the Main Page, but no detailed description was included in the extracted data; inferred to be the main page for Wikidata.

5. URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia
   - Link text/title: "Wikipedia" (this title appears in the extracted Main Page text: "Welcome to Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia").
   - Description: The visit extract contains the phrase "Welcome to Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia that anyone can edit," so this page is the Wikipedia article describing Wikipedia itself.

6. URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:About
   - Link text/title: not explicitly shown as separate text in the extract (inferred from URL: "Wikipedia:About"); however the Main Page extract does include the phrase "About Wikipedia" in the menu.
   - Description: The Main Page extract includes "About Wikipedia" in the navigation; no further descriptive text for this specific link was present in the extracted data. Inferred: an about page describing Wikipedia.

7. URL: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki
   - Link text/title: not explicitly provided in the extracted data (inferred from URL: "Wikidata").
   - Description: The visit extract lists Wikidata among related project links; the extract did not include a descriptive sentence for this link itself, so the description is inferred from its presence as a Wikidata link.

8. URL: https://en.wikiversity.org/wiki/Wikiversity:Main_Page
   - Link text/title: not explicitly provided in the extracted data (inferred from URL: "Wikiversity:Main_Page").
   - Description: The Main Page extract lists "Wikiversity" among "In other projects"; no page-specific descriptive text was provided in the extract, so this is inferred to be the Wikiversity main page.

9. URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Introduction_to_Wikipedia
   - Link text/title: not explicitly provided in the extracted data (inferred from URL: "Help:Introduction_to_Wikipedia").
   - Description: The extracted link list includes this help page, but the visit extract did not include a descriptive blurb; inferred to be the introductory help page for new users of Wikipedia.

10. URL: https://species.wikimedia.org/wiki/Main_Page
   - Link text/title: not explicitly provided in the extracted data (inferred from URL: "Wikispecies Main Page"); the visit extract does include "Wikispecies" in the "In other projects" list.
   - Description: The Main Page extract lists "Wikispecies" among other Wikimedia projects; no additional descriptive text for this specific link was present in the extract, so it is inferred to be the main page for Wikispecies.

Notes about evidence and missing items:
- All 10 full URLs above were present in the visit extract's "links" array. The visit result used as the source is: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page (this is the visit URL cited for the extracted link list).
- The merged visit data provided the URLs but rarely provided explicit link text/title or page descriptions for each link. Where the exact link text/title or descriptive text was not present in the extracted data, I explicitly noted that it was not provided and gave a clear inference from the URL. I did not visit the target pages; descriptions beyond what was in the extract were explicitly labeled as "inferred."
output.action_summary: I extracted 10 links from the Wikipedia Main Page visit results (source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page). For each link I listed the full URL, indicated whether the visit extract provided an explicit link text/title (and if not, gave an inferred title from the URL), and provided a 1-2 sentence description based only on the extracted data (or clearly marked as inferred when the extract lacked a description). If you want me to follow each URL and fetch the actual page titles and canonical 1-2 sentence descriptions, I can visit them next and provide verified titles and summaries (that would require visiting each target page).

0838924c-2412-4cb2-89e4-9030773f7643.title: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.
(

)

... that

... that only 42 votes separated the two major parties in the

?
... that the space-combat simulation game

was cancelled for poor quality despite the release of demo and beta versions and reviews?
... that when finished in 1947, the
Marcel Breuer House II
was suspended from cables costing US$22?
... that
Gabriele Lösekrug-Möller
worked as a bookseller before entering politics?
... that the
1927 Salvadoran coup attempt
failed after one of its leaders was unable to convince the president that he had been overthrown?
... that Reiji Okii came up with the title of
Mr. Noone Special
in the shower?

About
Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a 9th-century Christian
neo-Byzantine style
, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.
Photograph credit:
Diego Delso
Rece

merged_results.title: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and produce final entries

result.title: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and produce final entries

details.intent: From the extracted candidate links, choose 10 representative internal article links and compose for each: (A) the exact URL (converted to full https if needed but note original href), (B) the link text/title, and (C) a concise 1-2 sentence description based primarily on the Main Page context snippet or the link title if no snippet is present. Prefer diversity (current events, featured article, biography, science, culture).
selection_criteria: Diversity across topics (at least 4 different areas: e.g., geography/history/science/culture/current events)
Prefer links with clear nearby context_snippet on the Main Page
If many similar links exist, choose the one with the clearest description
details.description_method: Form descriptions using (in order of preference): (1) the context_snippet extracted from the Main Page (paraphrased into 1-2 sentences), (2) the anchor's title attribute if present, or (3) the link text if no other context is available. If a candidate's context is ambiguous, mark the description as 'summary from Main Page context; verify on article page if precise detail required.'
measurable_output_fields: final_links: [{ original_href: string, resolved_url: string, link_text: string, description: string (1-2 sentences), source_of_description: 'main_page_context'|'anchor_title'|'link_text' }]
verification_steps: Ensure each resolved_url corresponds to the original href (if relative '/wiki/...' resolve to 'https://en.wikipedia.org' + href, but keep original_href recorded).
Check that descriptions are 1-2 sentences and reference only information visible in the captured context or the anchor title.
Confirm there are no duplicates in final_links.
details.rationale: A think step here selects the best 10 links and creates concise descriptions grounded in on-page evidence. This avoids unverified guesses about article content while producing the exact outputs requested (URL, link text/title, short description).
details.goal: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and produce final entries
details.original_goal: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and produce final entries
details.parent_goal: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.

result.thinking_content: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and produce final entries

merged_results.title: Save final list of 10 links and descriptions to memory

32d56584-6a95-4e3b-a448-fcb0803ca44b.title: Visit Wikipedia Main Page and extract candidate links

details.intent: Fetch the Wikipedia Main Page HTML and extract a set of candidate internal links (URLs and link text) plus any nearby descriptive text so we can produce 10 link entries with brief descriptions without visiting each target page separately unless necessary.
target_facts: List of anchor elements that link to Wikipedia articles (hrefs beginning with /wiki/ or full https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/...) found on the page

For each anchor: the exact href string as found, the visible link text, and up to 200 characters of surrounding text/context on the Main Page (e.g., sentence/lede around the link) and any title="..." attribute on the anchor
details.extraction_plan: Load the Main Page at the provided URL. Search the main content areas (e.g., elements under id="content" or id="mp-" sections) for anchor (<a>) tags that link to internal articles. Filter out non-article links (namespaces such as Special:, Help:, File:, Category:, Talk:). For each candidate anchor capture: (1) exact href attribute as found on the page (if relative, capture the exact '/wiki/...' string), (2) link text, (3) any title attribute on the anchor, and (4) up to ~200 characters of surrounding visible text or the nearest summary paragraph to use as a short description source. Gather at least 20 candidate links so there's choice when selecting the final 10.
measurable_output_fields: candidates: [{ href_exact: string, link_text: string, link_title_attr: string|null, context_snippet: string|null, dom_path: string }]
verification_steps: Verify each href appears in the page HTML and begins with '/wiki/' or 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/' (internal article).
Confirm excluded namespaces were filtered (no Special:, Help:, File:, Category:, Talk:).
Ensure context_snippet is non-empty for those links that have a nearby descriptive sentence.
details.rationale: This node provides authoritative, on-page link URLs and any available contextual descriptions so the subsequent step can produce accurate 1-2 sentence descriptions without needing to visit every linked article. Capturing the exact href strings from the page prevents constructing or guessing URLs later and provides verifiable evidence.
details.goal: Visit Wikipedia Main Page and extract candidate links
details.original_goal: Visit Wikipedia Main Page and extract candidate links
details.parent_goal: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.

action_result.intent: Fetch the Wikipedia Main Page HTML and extract a set of candidate internal links (URLs and link text) plus any nearby descriptive text so we can produce 10 link entries with brief descriptions without visiting each target page separately unless necessary.
vector_context: Merge: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.
(

)

Did you know ...
Fanny Cochrane Smith
... that recordings of the voice of
Fanny Cochrane Smith
(pictured)
were used in the development of a modern Aboriginal Tasmanian constructed language?
... that
The Missing
took three years to write but only four days to film?
... that
Chicago State Cougars football
is the only
NCAA Division I
gridiron football program in Chicago?
... that only 42 votes separated the two major parties in the
2021 Liechtenstein general election
?
... that the space-combat simulation game
Renegade II
was cancelled for poor quality despite the release of demo and beta versions and reviews?
... that when finished in 1947, the
Marcel Breuer House II
was suspended from cables costing US$22?
... that
Gabriele Lösekrug-Möller
worked as a bookseller before entering politics?
... that the
1927 Salvadoran coup attempt
failed after one of its leaders was unable to convince the president that he had been overthrown?
... that Reiji Okii came up with the title of
Mr. Noone Special
in the shower?

About
Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a 9th-century Christian
neo-Byzantine style
, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.
Photograph credit:
Diego Delso
Rece

41b519ea-6825-4985-8ae0-6c7c06a2dc4d.title: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and produce final entries

details.intent: From the extracted candidate links, choose 10 representative internal article links and compose for each: (A) the exact URL (converted to full https if needed but note original href), (B) the link text/title, and (C) a concise 1-2 sentence description based primarily on the Main Page context snippet or the link title if no snippet is present. Prefer diversity (current events, featured article, biography, science, culture).
selection_criteria: Diversity across topics (at least 4 different areas: e.g., geography/history/science/culture/current events)
Prefer links with clear nearby context_snippet on the Main Page
If many similar links exist, choose the one with the clearest description
details.description_method: Form descriptions using (in order of preference): (1) the context_snippet extracted from the Main Page (paraphrased into 1-2 sentences), (2) the anchor's title attribute if present, or (3) the link text if no other context is available. If a candidate's context is ambiguous, mark the description as 'summary from Main Page context; verify on article page if precise detail required.'
measurable_output_fields: final_links: [{ original_href: string, resolved_url: string, link_text: string, description: string (1-2 sentences), source_of_description: 'main_page_context'|'anchor_title'|'link_text' }]
verification_steps: Ensure each resolved_url corresponds to the original href (if relative '/wiki/...' resolve to 'https://en.wikipedia.org' + href, but keep original_href recorded).
Check that descriptions are 1-2 sentences and reference only information visible in the captured context or the anchor title.
Confirm there are no duplicates in final_links.
details.rationale: A think step here selects the best 10 links and creates concise descriptions grounded in on-page evidence. This avoids unverified guesses about article content while producing the exact outputs requested (URL, link text/title, short description).
details.goal: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and produce final entries
details.original_goal: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and produce final entries
details.parent_goal: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.

79109cd1-5dff-45d1-a09b-0489ca34d4b2.title: Save final list of 10 links and descriptions to memory

details.intent: Persist the final list of 10 link entries (URL, link text/title, 1-2 sentence description, source_of_description) so it can be returned and referenced later.
details.data_to_save: final_links array as produced by the previous step plus metadata: source_page='https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page', extracted_at: timestamp.
details.rationale: Saving the final structured results enables reproducibility and quick retrieval if the user asks for the list again or follow-ups (like visiting specific links for more detail).
